title: Tassa Uttarī (Kāyotsarga-introducer)
summary: Tassa Uttarī is the short formula a practitioner recites to declare *why* they are about to stand in kāyotsarga — the meditative 'abandonment of the body.' Having just confessed harm to living beings in Iriyāvahiyaṁ, the reciter states that this stillness is undertaken for higher refinement, for atonement, for cleansing, for drawing out the inner 'thorns,' and ultimately for the destruction of sinful karmas — and ends with the resolve 'I undertake kāyotsarga.' It is one of the shortest sūtras of the ritual, yet it supplies the entire intention that gives the following silent meditation its meaning.
ritualPlacement: Tassa Uttarī belongs to the fifth Āvaśyaka, Kāyotsarga (Kāussagga). In the Devasi (base) sequence it comes immediately after Iriyāvahiyaṁ (the confession for harm to jīvas while moving) and is followed at once by Annattha (which lists the bodily exceptions that do not break the meditation); the two together frame the kāyotsarga that is then measured in Logassa-counts. It is a shared sūtra, recited identically across all five pratikramaṇas (Devasi, Rāi, Pakkhī, Cāumāsī, Sāṁvatsarī) — wherever a kāyotsarga is begun. Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī have no independent text of their own: they follow the Pakkhī procedure with only the word-substitution (pakkhī → cāumāsī / saṁvatsarī) and a longer kāyotsarga (20 Logassa / 40 Logassa + 1 Navkar), so this same formula simply recurs there unchanged.
authorAndSource: — (No individual author. Tassa Uttarī is an ancient Ardhamāgadhī Āvaśyaka/Prakrit ritual formula transmitted within the Śvetāmbara canon as part of the kāyotsarga procedure; no composer is named in the source book *Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit* (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition). The kāyotsarga breath-counts that govern its use are cited in-book from Āvaśyaka Niryukti gāthā 1532.)
structureArc: The sūtra is a single sentence (one verse). It unfolds as five instrumental phrases naming the purpose of the act, climaxing in a verb of resolve: (a) tassa uttarīkaraṇeṇaṁ — 'by way of its higher refinement / carrying-further' of the foregoing confession; (b) pāyacchittakaraṇeṇaṁ — 'by way of making atonement (prāyaścitta)'; (c) visohīkaraṇeṇaṁ — 'by way of purification (viśodhi)'; (d) visallīkaraṇeṇaṁ — 'by way of de-thorning,' removing the inner śalya (darts of deceit, craving, false belief); (e) pāvāṇaṁ kammāṇaṁ nigghāyaṇaṭṭhāe — 'for the purpose of the destruction of sinful karmas.' It then resolves: ṭhāmi kāussaggaṁ — 'I take my stand in kāyotsarga.' The four -karaṇa phrases form a deliberate progression from outward correction toward inward eradication, all subordinated to the final karmic goal.
keyThemes: Removal of the inner 'thorns' (śalya) of the soul
Destruction of sinful karma as the ultimate aim
Kāyotsarga as bodily abandonment / disciplined stillness

glossary.definition: Lit. 'abandonment of the body'; the practice of standing or sitting motionless, detached from bodily concerns, while meditating — the fifth Āvaśyaka. It is the act this sūtra introduces, and its duration is counted in Logassa recitations.
'Carrying further / higher refinement' — performing an additional act that completes and elevates the confession just made, so that nothing left over goes unaddressed.
Atonement or expiation; the prescribed corrective penance by which a fault is repaired (Prakrit pāyacchitta).
Cleansing or purification of the soul from the stain left by transgression (Prakrit visohī).

'De-thorning' — the drawing-out of the śalya, the inner darts lodged in the soul (classically the three: māyā/deceit, nidāna/craving for reward, mithyātva/false belief), so that no concealed barb obstructs progress (Prakrit visallīkaraṇa).
A 'thorn' or 'dart' — a hidden, festering flaw within the soul; the three śalyas are deceit, the craving-for-fruit, and wrong belief.
Sinful / demeritorious karma; the bondage that the practitioner aims to destroy (nigghāyaṇa) through this act (Prakrit pāvāṇaṁ kammāṇaṁ).
Striking down, destruction, eradication — here the destruction of accumulated sinful karmas, stated as the purpose (-aṭṭhāe = arthe, 'for the sake of') of the kāyotsarga.
The immediately preceding sūtra, a confession (micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ) for harm done to living beings while moving about — the fault that Tassa Uttarī now resolves to purify.
The sūtra recited right after Tassa Uttarī, listing the involuntary bodily acts (breathing, coughing, etc.) that are permitted and do not break the kāyotsarga.
One of the six daily obligatory observances; Tassa Uttarī serves the fifth, Kāyotsarga.

howToRead: A newcomer can read Tassa Uttarī simply as the practitioner announcing, in one breath, the five good reasons they are about to stand still in meditation, ending with 'so I now undertake kāyotsarga.' An advanced practitioner will weigh the deliberate ladder of the four -karaṇa terms — refinement → atonement → purification → de-thorning — as stages of inner work culminating in nirghātana of pāpa-karma, and will note that the same formula opens every kāyotsarga across all five pratikramaṇas.

sources.title: Āvaśyaka Niryukti (gāthā 1532, cited in Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit for kāyotsarga breath-counts)
sources.consultedFor: Authoritative measure governing the kāyotsarga that this sūtra introduces (300/500/1008 breaths).

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (H. D. T. Sheth, Prakrit–Sanskrit dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Glosses of pāyacchitta, visohī, visallī, nigghāyaṇa and the -karaṇa derivations.

notes: The semantics of visallīkaraṇa rest on śalya = 'thorn/dart'; the classical three-śalya reading (māyā, nidāna, mithyātva) is the standard Śvetāmbara interpretation but is not spelled out in the source book itself, so it is offered as received tradition rather than an in-book citation. uttarīkaraṇa is variously rendered 'higher/further refinement' or 'additional act'; both are attested in commentary and are compatible here. Verbatim spelling and orthography follow the source book (e.g. કાઉસ્સગ્ગ); IAST transliterations are normalized for the glossary. URLs for the cited print sources are intentionally left blank rather than fabricated.
